planting.note: Divide established mats of rhizomes in spring or fall and press the rhizomes firmly onto the surface of rocky outcrops, walls, or peaty substrates in shade; American wall fern is naturally epiphytic — it grows on bare rock surfaces, ledges, and tree bark rather than in deep soil. Tuck the rhizomes into moss or humus in rock crevices and keep moist until established. It does not grow well in ordinary garden soil.

directions: Plant in rocky, mossy, or peaty substrates on ledges, walls, or rock surfaces in full to part shade; American wall fern is a charming, small, leathery native fern that grows naturally on shaded rock outcrops, cliff ledges, boulders, and the bark of old trees — always on surfaces rather than in deep soil. It is one of the few truly epiphytic temperate ferns and forms spreading mats of narrow, once-pinnate, deep-green fronds from long, creeping surface rhizomes. The fronds are evergreen in mild climates and semi-evergreen where winters are colder, providing year-round interest on shaded rock surfaces where little else can grow. An invaluable fern for the rock garden, stone wall, or any shaded, rocky feature where its unique habitat preference can be accommodated. It grows very slowly but forms charming, permanent mats once established in suitable conditions. Native throughout northeastern North America south to the Appalachians, it is the eastern counterpart of the western licorice fern (Polypodium glycyrrhiza).

planting.note: Divide crowns in early spring or plant container-grown plants in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in part to full shade; spinulose wood fern tolerates a range of moisture conditions from consistently moist to moderately dry under tree canopy once established. It is one of the most widely distributed ferns in the temperate Northern Hemisphere and adaptable to a wide range of shaded garden conditions.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in part to full shade; spinulose wood fern is one of the most common and widely distributed woodland ferns in the temperate Northern Hemisphere, found across North America, Europe, and Asia in moist, deciduous and coniferous forests. The medium-sized, triangular, lacy fronds are elegantly divided two or three times, with small, sharply toothed (spinulose) segments that give the fronds a fine, delicate texture distinguishing them from the coarser Dryopteris filix-mas. The plant forms vase-shaped clumps of light-green to medium-green fronds that are semi-evergreen in mild winters. It is one of the most adaptable, tolerant, and widely planted ferns for woodland gardens, succeeding in a wide range of shade and moisture conditions. It hybridizes freely with other Dryopteris species, including producing the common hybrid D. x complexa with D. affinis. An excellent, dependable fern for naturalizing in woodland settings and for combining with hostas, hellebores, and woodland wildflowers.

planting.note: Plant container-grown or bare-root trees in moist, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; golden chain tree grows naturally on alkaline limestone soils and tolerates chalky conditions better than most ornamental trees. The hybrid 'Vossii' produces the longest pendulous chains of flowers and sets fewer viable seeds than the species, reducing the risk of seed toxicity. ALL PARTS ARE HIGHLY TOXIC if ingested — keep away from children and pets.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; golden chain tree is one of the most spectacular of all spring-flowering trees, producing long, pendant racemes of brilliant, clear-yellow pea flowers up to 24 inches in length in May and June — so dense and vividly colored that the entire tree appears to drip with cascades of golden chains, from which the common name derives. The famous Laburnum Walk at Bodnant Garden in Wales — a 180-foot tunnel of intertwined laburnums, trained overhead to create a tunnel of golden chains — demonstrates the full theatrical potential of this tree. In smaller gardens, a single mature specimen in full bloom against a dark background or blue sky is extraordinary. Native to central Europe and tolerant of shallow, chalky soils and part shade. The hybrid 'Vossii' is the most commonly planted, with the longest flower chains. EXTREME CAUTION: all parts of the plant, especially the seed pods and seeds, contain cytisine (a toxic alkaloid similar to nicotine) and are highly poisonous — the plant should never be planted where children play.

planting.note: Plant container-grown or bare-root plants in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Siberian pea tree is exceptionally cold-hardy and drought-tolerant and thrives in lean, alkaline conditions where most shrubs fail. It is one of the most cold-tolerant of all nitrogen-fixing leguminous shrubs and is a standard permaculture and shelterbelt species for the northern Great Plains and prairie provinces.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Siberian pea tree is one of the most cold-hardy and drought-tolerant nitrogen-fixing shrubs available, native to Siberia and northern China and reliably hardy to zone 2 — surviving temperatures of -50°F — making it invaluable for the most extreme northern climates where few other leguminous shrubs survive. The yellow pea flowers in spring are attractive and nectar-rich, providing early-season bee forage. The seed pods (up to 2 inches long) contain small seeds that are technically edible — they were eaten by indigenous Siberian peoples as a protein source in famine conditions — and are eaten by birds and small mammals. In permaculture design, Siberian pea tree serves as a cold-hardy nitrogen-fixing component for food forests, shelter belts, and northern homesteads. It is widely used for windbreaks and shelterbelt plantings on the northern Great Plains of North America and the prairie provinces of Canada. The weeping form 'Pendula' is sometimes grown as an ornamental grafted specimen.

planting.note: Scarify seeds before sowing in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; astragalus develops a deep taproot from which the medicinal dried root is harvested — roots are typically dug in the fall of the 3rd or 4th year, when the root is at maximum potency. As a nitrogen-fixing legume, it improves poor soils. Avoid heavy, wet soils which cause root rot.

directions: Sow scarified seeds in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; astragalus (huang qi, huang qi root) is one of the most important medicinal plants in Chinese medicine — used for over 2,000 years as a primary tonic herb for immune system support, energy, and longevity. The dried, sliced root (huang qi) appears in countless traditional Chinese medicine formulas and is increasingly studied in Western medicine for its immunomodulatory, adaptogenic, and antioxidant properties, including possible effects on telomerase activation. The plant itself is an attractive legume producing pinnate leaves on erect stems and small, pale-yellow pea flowers in spring. The root (used medicinally) is harvested in year 3–4 and dried into the characteristic yellow sticks sold in Chinese herb shops. As a legume, it fixes nitrogen and tolerates poor, dry soils. Now increasingly grown as a medicinal garden plant in North America as interest in traditional Chinese herbs grows.
